reasoning: Exchange rates over short horizons closely adhere to a random walk, which sets the base rate for a directional increase at roughly 0.50. However, the USD/MXN exchange rate has demonstrated a clear, persistent downtrend over both the past month (dropping from ~18.00 in early January to 17.35 by January 30) and the past year. Market consensus, supported by recent analyst commentary, expects the US Federal Reserve to continue cutting interest rates in early 2026. This is expected to maintain or widen the interest rate differential in favor of the higher-yielding Mexican peso, exerting further downward pressure on the USD/MXN pair. While the last three days of January showed a minor corrective bounce (17.23 to 17.35), the broader momentum and carry-trade dynamics lean bearish for the pair. Therefore, the probability of an increase over the next week is estimated slightly below 50%. As the forecast horizon extends to one month, directional predictability decreases and uncertainty grows; thus, the probability for the March 3 resolution date regresses closer to the 0.50 random-walk baseline.
